ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general nigiri sushi class (FOODON_00002809); no eel-specific nigiri subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers nigiri sushi broadly; no unagi-specific nigiri code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for unagi nigiri or nigiri sushi as a food item.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One piece of unagi nigiri weighs approximately 40 g, comprising roughly 15–18 g of grilled eel and 22–25 g of seasoned sushi rice.

nutrient_source.title: Unagi Nigiri — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (cr_id F05100149)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo record F05100149 for Unagi nigiri. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked Japanese eel (NDB 15033) and sushi rice composite, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for unagi nigiri as a composite dish. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: sushi rice (white short-grain Japanese rice, cooked), which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The eel and tare sauce contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (72 × 12.2 g available carbohydrate per 40 g serving) / 100 = 8.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 330 mg/100 g from tare glaze and seasoned sushi rice, with low potassium offset (~160 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit unagi nigiri to 2–3 pieces per meal, avoid additional soy sauce dipping, and request reduced tare glaze when ordering, as the combined sodium from the glaze and seasoned rice can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of 1500–2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI sushi rice (GI ~72) with 30.5 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 1.17 g dietary fibre/100 g, producing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict unagi nigiri to 2–3 pieces per sitting to limit carbohydrate load to approximately 12–18 g, pair with non-starchy accompaniments such as edamame or miso soup to slow glucose absorption, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely given the high glycemic index of the sushi rice base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium (330 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (~45 mg/100 g) offset partially by omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin D from grilled eel
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, unagi nigiri can be consumed in moderation (2–3 pieces) as the grilled eel provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin D; however, the sodium content from tare sauce and the refined carbohydrate load of sushi rice warrant portion control and avoidance of additional soy sauce to minimise blood pressure and glycaemic burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi nigiri (うなぎにぎり) is a classic Japanese sushi preparation consisting of a slice of grilled freshwater eel (Anguilla japonica) placed atop a hand-pressed oval of seasoned sushi rice. The eel is typically kabayaki-style: butterflied, skewered, steamed, then grilled over charcoal or a gas flame, and lacquered with a sweet-savoury tare glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The result is a deeply caramelised, smoky exterior with a tender, fatty interior. The sushi rice is short-grain Japanese rice seasoned with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, giving it a slightly sweet, tangy flavour and a sticky, cohesive texture. Per 100 g, unagi nigiri provides 210 kcal, 6.75 g protein, 6.6 g fat, and 30.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is 330 mg/100 g, primarily from the tare glaze and seasoned rice. The eel contributes omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin D (~2.5 mcg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (~1.2 mcg/100 g), making it nutritionally distinctive among sushi varieties. The glycemic index is high (~72) due to the refined sushi rice, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for unrestricted consumption by those with type 2 diabetes or hypertension, but the omega-3 and vitamin D content offer cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits when consumed in moderation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in unagi nigiri?
How much sodium is in eel sushi?
What is the GI of sushi rice?
Is unagi nigiri high in fat?
How many pieces of unagi nigiri per serving?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from HPB Foodo canonical record F05100149 (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, micronutrients, and fatty acid profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15033 (cooked Japanese eel) and sushi rice composite, which are approved secondary sources. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using sushi rice as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, a well-documented analogous approach.
notes: Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (2.9 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g/100 g), and micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins, minerals, water) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15033 (Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) and a sushi rice composite, as HPB Foodo does not report these fields for F05100149. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g (confirmed negligible for grilled eel and sushi rice per USDA data). GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white short-grain Japanese sushi rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct composite GI value for unagi nigiri exists in approved sources. The rheumatoid arthritis suitability assessment from the input record contained an internal contradiction (the reason stated omega-3 benefits but the conclusion was unsuitable); this has been resolved in the health_context section by focusing on the three mandatory conditions. Halal status is marked false as traditional tare sauce contains mirin (rice wine) and sake, which are not halal-certified in standard preparations.
